note: Each archetype includes: roast-tier longDesc, result-page quote, svgPortrait description, portraitPrompt (for AI image gen).

entries.name: The Waterboy
entries.shortDesc: Your relationship with basketball is maintained mostly by friends jamming a quiz link in your face.
entries.quote: Tip-off already? I thought scrolling was how you redeem DoorDash credits.
entries.longDesc:
Congrats. The system flipped through 25 fan archetypes and not a single one wanted to claim you. You were probably dragged here by a friend, or you clicked the link thinking it was a Nike SNKRS restock. Everyone else is reminiscing about 35 seconds and 13 points, posting anniversary threads for 58, lighting candles for J.R. Smith — you're trying to remember whether that halftime ad had a promo code.

The system tried, honestly. It asked you about pick-and-rolls, dunks, logo threes, locker-room drama — you answered like someone filling out a survey in a language you half-recognize. Your most stable relationship with basketball is noticing a park game is too loud on your way past.

But look, you showed up. Somebody has to warm the end of the bench, and you finished all 25 questions, which technically makes you more committed than a lot of "Finals only" fans. You earned your seat — it's the last row, facing the exit, but it's yours.

The only thing the system noted with mild respect: you didn't bounce at question 12 when the vocabulary got weird. That's more endurance than some bandwagon accounts. If this were a two-way contract, you wouldn't make the roster, but you'd get a nice handshake. Respect.
entries.svgPortrait: A person in an obviously too-big jersey sits at the very end of a bench, both hands clutching a bubble tea and a phone, staring off to the side completely checked out. Behind them, a wall of silhouetted fans goes wild. A few unopened programs are on the floor. Mood: out of place, unbothered, random passerby. Palette: cool grays and blues, with basketball orange only on the background fans for contrast.
entries.portraitPrompt: Humorous cartoon-style basketball bystander, oversized jersey in the corner of a bench, bubble tea and phone in hand, clearly zoned out, roaring fans behind them, orange-black arena backdrop, cool gray-blue tones emphasizing the disconnect.

entries.name: The Six-Pack Spectator
entries.shortDesc: The game is the snack. Timeouts are refills. Fourth quarter is a shouting match with your TV.
entries.quote: Don't tip off yet — I need to crack another one.
entries.longDesc:
You don't watch games, you run an alcohol-powered program. Warmups = open the first one. Timeouts = refill. Halftime = raise a glass to your younger self. Fourth quarter = you, the screen, the Twitter timeline, the refs, and an imaginary rival fan all yelling at once.

It's not that you don't know ball. Your first-quarter analysis is honestly pretty sharp. The problem starts in Q2, when the ethanol takes over your prefrontal cortex and your discussion of hedge-and-recover strategy gets autocompleted into "THIS REF IS BLIND" and a dialect-specific stream of words that can't be repeated on this page. By the end of Q3, you've forgotten the score, but your vocal cords remember the damage.

The next morning you piece it together: did we win? Did we lose? Did I yell at the ref? Did I almost yeet the remote at the TV? A friend sends you screenshots of the group chat at 2:14am — voice notes, 16 of them, every one over 30 seconds, tonally ranging from euphoric to furious to philosophical to blackout — basically a compressed documentary of human emotion.

You don't regret a thing. The playoffs come back around and you know exactly what you'll do. The beer is already cold.
entries.svgPortrait: A person in an unzipped jersey stands in a cluttered living room in front of the TV, one hand raising a beer bottle, the other jabbing at the screen mid-yell. The coffee table is covered in empty cans, snacks, and a phone. The TV shows a blurry live game. His face is flushed, mouth wide open. A kicked-over slipper on the floor. A crooked team flag tacked to the wall. Palette: warm yellow light, basketball orange, beer gold.
entries.portraitPrompt: Exaggerated cartoon-style drunk basketball fan, unzipped team jersey, standing in living room in front of TV, beer raised, yelling, flushed face, crushed cans and snacks on table, messy cheer gear, warm amber lighting.

entries.name: The GOAT Arbiter
entries.shortDesc: Every basketball thread in your orbit ends with a freshly re-ranked top ten.
entries.quote: Hold off on the hot take — let me re-rank the top ten first.
entries.longDesc:
Your entire reason for logging on is issuing rulings about all-time hierarchy. Skip Bayless said "consensus? according to whom?" once and you thought the man was thinking too small — you don't just question consensus, you define it.

Someone posts a poster dunk in the group chat and your first reaction isn't "filthy" — it's "does this clip count toward top-50 all-time posters?" Someone hypes a current star and you instantly reflex into era adjustments, rule differences, strength of opposition, ring quality, FMVP share — like an automated historical-tribunal bot booting up.

Your strongest feature is that you actually do the work. There are at least three versions of an "all-time rankings" spreadsheet on your machine, each with different weights — rings, advanced stats, a third one called "vibes adjusted" which is just your gut but with a respectable-looking column next to it.

Your weakness is that you can't stop. Postgame interviews become three-paragraph reads on "what this quote reveals about his anxiety over his legacy." Your bookmarks bar has 37 GOAT threads and a different top-ten under each one, because your board gets revised monthly.

People find you exhausting. You think they're just under-leveled. You're both right, but your version of right is usually 3,000 words long and comes with a link to Basketball Reference.
entries.svgPortrait: A stern person stands in front of a massive whiteboard crowded with player names and ranking numbers, some crossed out, some with arrows repositioning them. Red marker in hand, revising live. Binders and printed stat sheets stacked on a table. GOAT posters on the wall, each with their own ranking sticker. Palette: orange accent lighting, dark background, bright whiteboard as focal point.
entries.portraitPrompt: Comic poster-style sports pundit fan, standing in a room covered in GOAT posters, holding binders and a red marker, whiteboard of rankings behind, intense and authoritative expression, warm orange lighting, printed stat sheets scattered.

entries.name: The Brick Prophet
entries.shortDesc: Still in the third quarter and you're already producing your own clown-yourself content.
entries.quote: Lock it in — hold on, let me delete this tweet first.
entries.longDesc:
You're the Westbrook of hot-take volume — the most attempts in the league, abysmal hit rate, infinite confidence.

Before tip you're a prophet: "My team by 30." "Sweep." "50-burger tonight." Your delivery is calm, eyes steady, like you're reading out a verdict. Then the game starts. The first-quarter cracks appear. By the fourth the prophet voice has quietly morphed into "I mean, I had concerns earlier."

In the group chat you function as a personal clowning factory — you generate the flag, you get clowned on the flag. Everyone there has learned: once you type "locked in," somebody should screenshot. They call it "popping the champagne," as in: you pop, they screenshot.

Remember the 2018 WCF Game 7 Rockets, 0-for-27 from three? That's not a stat, that's your spirit glyph. It's not about Houston specifically — it's about the cosmic embarrassment of being the team that should have won and instead just kept bricking. You've turned that energy into a lifestyle.

The terrifying part is you never learn. Next game your first group chat message will still be "we got this tonight." Because in your philosophy, getting clowned isn't shameful — not predicting is. There's something oddly Mamba about that. The difference is Kobe made his. You did not.
entries.svgPortrait: A person stands in front of a giant scoreboard showing a brutal loss for their team, one hand holding up a screenshot of a bold pre-game prediction ("MY TEAM BY 30!"), the other covering their face. Behind them, group chat screenshots project "LMAO" reactions. Their jersey number reads "0-27." Expression: stubborn embarrassment. Palette: red-orange dominant with awkward pink overlays.
entries.portraitPrompt: Exaggerated comedic fan, in jersey labeled 0-27, standing in front of scoreboard showing loss, holding up bold pre-game prediction screenshot, face partially covered, group chat roast reactions floating behind, stubborn embarrassed expression.

entries.name: The Process Truther
entries.shortDesc: A win feels like a lost pick. A loss feels like a deposit into next season.
entries.quote: The future is yours. Just don't win tonight.
entries.longDesc:
"Trust The Process" is not a slogan to you. It's a theology. You've elevated tanking into an actual belief system.

Most fans grieve losses. You celebrate them — sincerely, joyfully, with a warmth normally reserved for weddings. "Yesss another L, lottery odds looking healthy." You post about it like a proud parent. Worse: when your team accidentally wins one, you react like someone just short-sold your retirement account. "NO — you just cratered our draft position. Tonight is a historic disaster."

You read draft scouting reports like they're your company's annual filing. From March on, you can rattle off every projected top-10 pick's measurables, college splits, and NBA comp: "He's a Giannis cousin." "Zion burst but the knees are a watch-list item." You absorb the information with something bordering on parental affection for teenagers your team may never draft.

Your idols aren't players — they're the 2014-16 Sixers front office, performance art masquerading as an NBA franchise. Critics say it was embarrassing. You say it was avant-garde. Embiid kind of proved you right. Then kind of didn't.

In your fan group chat your nickname is "GM of the front office." You think that's a compliment.
entries.svgPortrait: A person kneels in front of a giant "LOTTERY" ball machine, hands pressed together in prayer, expression devout. Their old jersey is covered in printed photos of draft prospects. A "Trust The Process" banner hangs above. Table covered in scouting reports and draft board comps. Crossed-out season records litter the floor. Palette: blue-gray base, gold lottery balls, hint of hope-glow.
entries.portraitPrompt: Humorous comic-style rebuilding fan, old jersey, kneeling at a lottery ball machine in prayer, screen behind showing draft prospects and stats, Trust The Process banner overhead, fanatical devout expression.

entries.name: The Armchair Coach
entries.shortDesc: Your whiteboard diagrams outdraw 90% of the league. You still fumble the timeout signal.
entries.quote: Put me in — I mean, as the coach.
entries.longDesc:
You've never coached an actual game, but you've fired no less than fifty coaching staffs inside your own head.

During the broadcast, nobody works harder than you. The moment a timeout is called you stage a hostile takeover of the huddle: "SUB." "ZONE." "MISMATCH, POST HIM UP." "WHY NO HEDGE?!" In clutch moments you don't sit, you coach standing, scowling like Pop, stabbing at the screen, one clipboard toss short of an ejection. Your partner calls it your "coach syndrome." It's quarterly now.

Your Xs-and-Os game is real. You can sketch a respectable Horns set and Spanish pick-and-roll on a napkin. The problem is you've never had to run any of it on actual humans. If you were handed five live players, you'd discover quickly: live players do not obey. Live players dribble it off their foot, pass it into the third row, cut the wrong direction — and your beautiful whiteboard can't override a single hesitation dribble.

This does not stop you from dropping a "Tonight's Breakdown" post after every loss. Title template: "If I were coaching, we win by 15 minimum." You know it's fantasy. You also know fantasy is still legal on Reddit.

You see Pop in the mirror. The league sees a mid-season interim fired by All-Star break. Either way, your diagrams really are clean.
entries.svgPortrait: A person in a hoodie stands in front of their living room TV holding a whiteboard and a marker, drawing plays as the broadcast shows a paused timeout. Hand-drawn plays on napkins scatter the coffee table, a whistle and a remote in frame. Deadly serious expression, directing invisible players. An indifferent cat sits on the couch. Palette: dark blue-gray background, whiteboard white, basketball orange arrows.
entries.portraitPrompt: Comic-style armchair coach fan, hoodie, clutching whiteboard and marker in front of TV, paused-timeout broadcast, scribbled play diagrams, whistle on couch, intense expression directing invisible players, unbothered cat nearby.

entries.name: The Analytics Enforcer
entries.shortDesc: A cold-blooded spreadsheet machine who ends every basketball argument with a link.
entries.quote: Don't tell me what it felt like. TS% is the variable, not vibes.
entries.longDesc:
You've fully reclassified basketball as applied statistics. Everyone else is talking about "feel" and "killer instinct" and "he just reads the game." You respond with a screenshot of advanced stats and steamroll the vibes side into dust.

Your debate posture is blunt: PER, BPM, VORP, WS/48, RAPTOR, EPM — six columns, opponents still mid-sentence, you've already filed a verdict. You don't argue with words, you argue with Basketball Reference's page-load speed.

Your spiritual ancestor isn't a player — it's Daryl Morey. A GM who rewrote scouting via regression models. You consider him a prophet. You won't talk about 0-for-27 publicly but you felt it in your chest.

The catch is you may have forgotten basketball is a sport first and a dataset second. You don't watch games with your eyes, you watch them via browser tabs. A three-quarter performance you only saw on Synergy gets a full efficiency breakdown from you, accurate to two decimal places. The data has overwritten the game.

People complain: "talking ball with you is like talking quarterly reports." You don't take that as an insult. You take it as the correct frame finally reaching them.
entries.svgPortrait: A person in glasses sits at a desk with three wraparound monitors, each covered in basketball dashboards, line charts, and Excel grids. One hand holds coffee, the other points at a data spike — calm face with a hint of superiority. Shelves of stats books and printouts around them. Room is dark, only screens lighting the face. Palette: tech blue, data green, deep dark background.
entries.portraitPrompt: Tech-forward comic-style analytics fan, glasses, seated at multi-monitor setup, screens showing advanced basketball stats and spreadsheets, coffee in hand, cold confident expression, dark home office lit only by screens.

entries.name: Mr. 58-Point Benchmark
entries.shortDesc: Every blowout has to pass through your 58-point measuring stick first.
entries.quote: Only lost by 30? That's 0.52 of a 58. Not even a passing grade.
entries.longDesc:
You sit on the unofficial Basketball Measurement Council. In your universe, "58" isn't a score, it's a unit.

On January 11, 2009, Phoenix beat New Orleans 140-82. CP3 got cooked by 58 points in his own building. Since that night, your brain has been calibrated to a 58-point ruler. Any lopsided result must pass under it first:

"Lost by 25? Under half a 58. Embarrassing, but not even qualifying embarrassing."

"Won by 40? Still 18 shy of a 58, keep the trophy in the closet."

"Lost by 50? Closer. Still 8 short. Don't clout-chase."

You might not have watched that broadcast live — that's fine. You've quoted it for fifteen years. CP3 played for four teams and retired; the 58 will never retire. It's past the game. It's cultural patrimony, a standard, a flag. You hold the flag.

The thing you do most on the forum is calculate, to one decimal place, how many 58s a blowout is worth. People find you insufferable. You think the one thing basketball Twitter lacks is mathematical hygiene, and you're providing it as a public service.
entries.svgPortrait: A person stands beside a massive ruler where the largest mark is 58, starred and highlighted; smaller marks say 20, 30, 40. He holds a pointer at the 58 line, smug and focused. Behind him, a collage of blowout scoreboards. Shirt reads "0.52 of a 58." Palette: black base, gold tick marks, red score numbers.
entries.portraitPrompt: Ironic comic-style meme fan, holding a giant ruler with 58 starred, standing in front of a blowout scoreboard collage, smug expression, meme shirt, black background with gold ticks and red scores.

entries.name: The Bandwagon Ace
entries.shortDesc: Your team rotates with the title. You own more jerseys than anyone because you buy a new one every June.
entries.quote: I'm not chasing rings — I'm chasing the beauty of peak competition.
entries.longDesc:
Your fandom history reads like an NBA title list, in order. 2016: Cavs. 2017: Warriors. 2021: Bucks. 2023: Nuggets. Each June, the instant the Larry O'Brien is lifted, your avatar and cover photo switch with a glide transition faster than the free agency window.

You loathe the term "ring chaser." You prefer "appreciator of peak competitive basketball." Fancy phrasing. Same pattern. Winner-takes-you.

You are the spiritual heir of KD. He played for four teams. You've repped fourteen. The difference is KD can ball. You, meanwhile, have mastered one move: the pre-season declaration of "I'm not switching this time," followed five months later by a stealth Amazon order for the new champion's jersey.

Your closet is a living museum of NBA champions. Warriors, Cavs, Bucks, Nuggets — each jersey retired after one summer of wear. Friends dig up your old photos and realize you've been photographed in eight different teams' gear over the past decade, perfectly matched to that season's champion.

Some say you have no soul. You counter that you've had the richest fan experience possible. Technically, you're right. The cost is that no one ever believes you when you say "this one's my team."
entries.svgPortrait: A person layers five or six different team jerseys, each peeking out at the edges. Arms full of this year's champion merch — cap, scarf, flag. Old jerseys lie discarded on the floor. Confident, unbothered expression ("this is normal"). Multiple franchise championship silhouettes overlap in the background. Palette: rainbow collage, gold championship glow.
entries.portraitPrompt: Over-the-top cartoon-style bandwagon fan, layered multiple team jerseys, arms full of latest champion merch, old team caps tossed on the floor, unfazed smile, multiple title celebrations overlapping behind.

entries.name: The Kiss of Death
entries.shortDesc: A living reverse-jinx — say one thing clutch and the score flips.
entries.quote: Not my fault. Fate picked me. (inbounds pass to the other team.gif)
entries.longDesc:
You are the anti-rally towel. You aren't trying to curse your team — but every time you open your mouth in the last 90 seconds, the universe finds a way to collect.

You post "locked in." The other team scores four in a row. You buy a player's jersey. He tears his ACL the next week. You finally show up to a game in person. Your team sets a season low.

Your spirit play forever is J.R. Smith in Game 1 of the 2018 Finals. Tied game. Ran out the clock. He thought they were up. That look on his face when the buzzer went? That is your soul. Not malicious, not unprepared — just a clutch moment where the brain and the body briefly stopped talking to each other, on national TV.

Your friend group has built a whole containment protocol around you. No talking in clutch time. No memes. No screenshots. No posts in the fan chat. Each of your posts is another straw on a scale, and the other end of that scale is your team's playoff life. They've mapped it empirically. They respect it.

You package all of this as "I didn't mean to." Deep down you know the truth: at this point, it's your personal brand.
entries.svgPortrait: A person frozen in front of the TV, phone screen showing a just-sent "LOCKED IN" message. The broadcast score flips in real time — their team's points plummet. Expression: collapsed innocence. Ghost of the J.R. Smith frozen-face moment floats behind him. Group chat pings: "It's you again." Palette: melancholic gray-blue, red alert score, green chat bubbles.
entries.portraitPrompt: Humorous comic-style jinx fan, frozen mid-send of 'locked in' text as TV score reverses, devastated innocent face, JRSmith ghost silhouette behind, group chat 'shut up' pings floating, gray-blue with red alert.

entries.name: The Hater in Chief
entries.shortDesc: Your emotional KPIs are hard-coded to the team you hate most.
entries.quote: A win, sure — but look at their strength of schedule.
entries.longDesc:
Your on/off switch isn't wired to your own team. It's welded to the franchise (or star) you hate most. Their ring hurts more than your own losses. Their comeback wrecks you. Their MVP speech gives you insomnia. You are, functionally, a parasocial negative — you feed on their pain.

Your specialty is finding the flaw in other people's highlights. Ring? Weak conference. MVP? Voter bias. Game-winner? Defense asleep. You're a dedicated defect-detector, on call 24/7, positioned precisely where the celebrations happen so you can pour cold water.

The irony is you know that player's stat sheet better than his own fans. TS%, clutch splits, playoff vs. regular season gap — you have receipts ready. Because the hater workflow needs data. Your "reasons he's overrated" folder is deeper than the average stan's highlights folder.

You tell people you're "analytically honest." Everyone sees the salt. You're the guy who drops "ring quality" threads ten minutes after the Finals MVP is announced. You're not a bad person. You just dressed up envy in a lab coat and called it objective.
entries.svgPortrait: A person arms crossed in front of a TV broadcasting a rival's championship parade. Exaggerated scowl. Thought bubbles float around him: "weak schedule," "soft opponents," "asterisk ring." A notepad beside him is scribbled with negative stat lines about their star. Palette: sour yellow-green, cold gray base, glinting championship gold on-screen for contrast.
entries.portraitPrompt: Exaggerated comic-style salty fan, arms crossed scowling at TV championship parade, floating lemons and hot-take bubbles, notepad full of anti-star stats, sour yellow-green against gold trophy glow.

entries.name: The Ref Truther
entries.shortDesc: A loss is never a loss. It's always a whistle-shaped conspiracy.
entries.quote: Wait — let me slow this to 0.25 speed. See? His foot's on the line. Clearly.
entries.longDesc:
Your viewing rule is ironclad: wins are earned, losses are rigged. That loop has been running in your head for years without a single crash.

Every 50/50 call becomes a national-security-level deep dive. League office, officiating crew, betting markets, home-court advantage — you wire all four into one supply chain and you're the only person on earth brave enough to name it. You've emailed the league 47 times. Silence. To you, silence means guilty.

What you love most is the Last Two Minute Report. If the report concedes an error: "Officiating confirmed — told you." If the report says the call was right: "LTMR is a cover-up — look at who wrote it." You win either way. Your position isn't fact-based, it's faith-based.

Your phone has a folder called "Officiating Evidence": 400+ screenshots of disputed whistles, sorted by season, opponent, and crew chief. Friends think you're too deep. You think they're too asleep. Nobody changes their mind. That's because, deep down, you both kind of enjoy the fight.
entries.svgPortrait: A person clutching a remote stands in front of a TV frozen on a slow-motion controversial call, a red circle drawn over a foot on a line. Other hand holds a magnifying glass. Around him float whistles, red cards, and "RIGGED!" bubbles. Table scattered with printed LTMR pages. Palette: deep red indignation, black background, TV blue light.
entries.portraitPrompt: Comic-style outraged fan clutching remote and magnifier at TV with slow-mo controversial call, red circles on screen, whistles and 'rigged' bubbles floating, LTMR printouts on table.

entries.name: The Fire-Everyone Guy
entries.shortDesc: Every basketball problem in your universe resolves the same way: fire, trade, sell, reset.
entries.quote: Three losses in a row and the coach is STILL here? What are we waiting for — a sweep?
entries.longDesc:
In your solution space, everything reduces to one verb: fire. Coach underperforming? Fire. GM botched the draft? Fire. Star in a slump? Trade — which is just fire with paperwork. Owner cheap? Sell the team — the final-boss fire.

80% of your forum posts fit the template "___ has to go." The other 20% is "___ should be traded." Your problem-solving instinct is basically a reboot — don't ask what's wrong, just restart.

Your peak was a regular-season loss to the league's best team. A normal fan might've shrugged. You wrote an essay: "Tonight's Loss Exposes the Total Failure of the Coaching Staff." 27 views, 0 likes. You still thought you spoke truth to power.

You do have a whole alternate management plan. You can name who to sign, who to flip, who to draft. The snag is none of your proposals ever clear the cap sheet. That's fine. What matters is that you know you'd do the job better, and if there's ever a civilian GM competition, you're first in line.
entries.svgPortrait: A person on an arena concourse holds up two banners: left reads "COACH OUT," right reads "GM OUT." Mouth wide open mid-chant. Fans nearby are either taking photos or edging away. Background: losing streak headlines and a coach walking out of the tunnel with his head down. Crumpled trade-machine printouts at his feet. Palette: angry red, black background, sharp white text.
entries.portraitPrompt: Poster-style angry fan in arena concourse holding 'FIRE HIM' banners, yelling, fans photographing and avoiding him, background shows losing streak headlines, crumpled trade proposals underfoot, red dominant.

entries.name: The Highlight Disciple
entries.shortDesc: A game without a dunk is like hotpot without meat. Edible, but spiritually empty.
entries.quote: Defense? Schemes? Corner threes? I'm here for posters. Leave the rest to the coaches.
entries.longDesc:
Your basketball demands are simple: someone takes off, you got your money's worth. No one takes off, the game is a waste. Motion offense, help-side rotation, off-ball screens — those occupy the same slot in your basketball universe as vegetables do in hotpot: theoretically required, personally untouched.

Your Instagram saves are a full dunk encyclopedia. You can distinguish a windmill, tomahawk, reverse 360, and free-throw-line launch down to the half-rotation. You rank Dunk Contest scores with more rigor than the judges. But if someone asks who your team's starting point guard is, you'll freeze. "He's the… short one?"

The game type you despise most is a Grizzlies-Cavs 90-88 grinder — sub-20-point quarters, full-court halfcourt, methodical defense. To you, that's a subtitled foreign film. Probably good. You cannot watch it.

You remember Vince's 2000 Dunk Contest in crisper detail than your graduation. Some will tell you that doesn't count as "being a fan." You'll counter that the NBA literally invented the Slam Dunk Contest for you. Checkmate. Mostly checkmate.
entries.svgPortrait: A person tilts his head up at a giant silhouette mid-dunk in the sky — eyes blazing, mouth open, fists clenched. Phone tilted horizontally recording. Around him, the rest of a normal basketball play — pass, screen, off-ball run — is greyed out and blurred. Only the dunk frame is in full color. Palette: hot red-orange, high contrast, grayed-out tactical elements.
entries.portraitPrompt: High-energy comic-style dunk fan, head back at a giant silhouette dunking overhead, eyes glowing, filming on phone, rest of play grayed out, only dunk frame in full color, fiery red-orange palette.

entries.name: The Logo-Shot Evangelist
entries.shortDesc: Curry makes it: sacred. Anyone else: lucky. Standard issue biased lens.
entries.quote: That's not a three. When Curry takes it from there, it's a logo shot.
entries.longDesc:
You've sanctified the three-point shot. Steph Curry is your prophet.

Any three, anywhere, by anyone, gets routed back to him within three seconds: "See? Curry changed the whole league. There's no modern game without him." What you live for is the 0.8 seconds the ball is in the air — you already know. Certainty has become faith.

Your scale of judgment is comically biased. Curry deep three: art. Dame deep three: fine, but not Curry. Luka deep three: lucky. Role player deep three: "that's not a logo shot, that's chucking." Your standard isn't distance or difficulty, it's whose hands released the ball.

You've trained the release at the park: fast hop, high arc, one-legged step-back. The mechanics are honestly on-model. The only issue is your percentage is roughly 1/10 of his. You blame the surface, the wind, the rims. Not you.

Your phone wallpaper is the iconic "already walking away before the ball drops" Curry frame. You call it the most beautiful moment in basketball history. On this specific point, you may be right.
entries.svgPortrait: A person arms raised in worship, staring up at a basketball tracing an impossibly high arc toward a distant hoop. Logo at mid-court marked under their feet. Huge arena in the background with dramatic lights. Expression: devout awe. Clothed in number-30 color scheme. Palette: gold/blue (Warriors hint), orange ball trail, halo glow.
entries.portraitPrompt: Comic-style three-point evangelist, arms raised in worship, looking up at high-arc logo shot, logo marking on floor, massive arena stadium lighting, fervent devout expression, gold-blue with orange ball trail halo.

entries.name: The Buzzer-Beater Believer
entries.shortDesc: Late and down twelve? You're still not turning it off. Miracles are in theory possible.
entries.quote: Don't turn it off — 35 seconds, 13 points. Anything can happen.
entries.longDesc:
You're the last romantic left in basketball. You believe in comebacks, in miracles, in the last tick of the clock reshaping everything — even when win probability is somewhere near your ankle.

30 seconds to go, down 12, everyone else has already turned off the TV. You're still on the couch shouting, "They got time! 35 seconds, 13 points — remember?" That happened once. It happened in 2004. It happened to T-Mac. You are certain the second occurrence is tonight. Every tonight.

Your prized saves folder isn't dunks — it's game-winners. Reggie Miller's 8 points in 8.9 seconds against the Knicks. T-Mac's 35-and-13. Kyrie's Game 7 dagger. Dame's 0.9 over OKC. You loop them in your head more frequently than your company plays its quarterly deck.

People call you unrealistic. You call it basketball's romance. The meaning of watching isn't the margin, it's that one tick — ball spinning, every seat frozen — when time stops and everything is possible.

The issue is that tick arrives slightly less often in your life than 35-and-13 reoccurring. You still won't turn it off. That's romantic. It's also a little ridiculous. Both true.
entries.svgPortrait: A person, fists clenched, stands in front of a TV showing 0.5 seconds left and a brutal deficit. Expression is not despair — it's tight, hopeful anticipation, eyes bright. Behind him drift translucent ghost frames of T-Mac's 13-in-35, Reggie's 8-in-8.9, Dame's 0.9. Morning light already outside the window; he hasn't moved. Palette: dramatic warm orange, red score tension, glow.
entries.portraitPrompt: Romantic comic-style comeback believer, fists clenched facing TV with 0.5 seconds and deficit, hopeful intense expression, translucent legendary game-winner frames floating behind, dawn outside, dramatic warm orange lighting.

entries.name: The Ride-or-Die Martyr
entries.shortDesc: If the team folds, you go down with them — and you still won't switch.
entries.quote: I'm not leaving. They need me. (glances at the 30-loss streak)
entries.longDesc:
You and your team have a soul-level binding contract. Exit penalty: next lifetime. Team relocates? You move your group chat with them. Team rebrands? You update your handle. Team tanks for 20 years? You've been there 20 years. When a friend suggests you switch, you look at them the way J.R. Smith looked at that pass — profound confusion laid over profound love.

You roast them publicly. Coach, front office, draft eye, offensive efficiency — you drag them harder than any hater. You can, because you're the one who actually cares, actually watched every minute, actually hurt. But the second an outsider piles on, you flip: "Our squad, I get to criticize. You? Pipe down."

The scary part is your tolerance threshold. Your team is rubble and you'll still pull a "this year we got something" out of the debris. You've been saying "this year" for twelve straight years. You'll say it next year too. You call this loyalty. A therapist might call it Stockholm.

You own one jersey. Not for lack of funds — to you, buying another would be betrayal. That jersey has faded into an off-color relative of itself, the number barely legible, but you wear it proudly, because a new jersey would have no memory. The old one holds the bad seasons you survived together.
entries.svgPortrait: A person in a washed-out faded jersey stands alone outside a decaying arena. Letters missing from the arena sign. Holding a team scarf in both hands, expression tired but set. Behind him, a wall plastered with years of season ticket stubs and losing headlines — but the top one reads "this year." Palette: warm faded sepia, muted team colors, steady orange eye-light.
entries.portraitPrompt: Weighty comic-style diehard, faded team jersey standing outside worn-down arena holding scarf, behind him wall of ticket stubs and losing-season clippings with top one reading 'this year,' tired but resolute face, sepia tones.

entries.name: The Lone Wolf Spectator
entries.shortDesc: Watch alone. Break it down alone. Win every argument — alone — because there's no one to fight.
entries.quote: I watch alone and I enjoy it alone. Don't need anyone to co-sign.
entries.longDesc:
You watch basketball like it's a solo meditation. You don't want company, don't want to explain yourself, don't care what the group chat thinks. Not because you're empty-headed — you're quietly picky. You just don't think your takes belong in a group that underwhelms you.

When the chat erupts, you lurk. People assume you didn't watch. In fact you watched closer than anyone. You just figured your breakdown wasn't worth typing for that audience. "I know what I saw" has become so internalized it tips into mild snobbery.

You watch the 4am west-coast tip-off alone. No post. No group chat. No DM. Afterwards you might write 200 words in the Notes app — for yourself, not them. You've kept a "season log" folder since 2019 that no human has seen.

People call you aloof. Some call it haughty. In truth it's social anxiety stacked on top of filtering. Your bar for a watch-buddy is higher than your bar for a date, which means you've landed on the only reliable one: yourself.

Mamba spent 4am in the gym. You spend 4am alone in your living room. Spiritually, you think you two are similar. Physically, the distance is substantial.
entries.svgPortrait: A person sitting alone in a dark living room late at night, lit only by the blue-white of the TV and a faint phone glow. Leaned back on the couch. Expression calm and cool — neither hyped nor sad. A glass of water and an open Notes app on the coffee table. A 4am cityscape outside the window. Nobody else. Palette: deep navy, cold screen white, faint warm city lights.
entries.portraitPrompt: Cool-toned comic-style lone fan, alone late-night living room watching game, only TV and phone light, calm detached face, water glass and notes app on table, 4am skyline outside, undisturbed solitude.

entries.name: The Stat-Padding Auditor
entries.shortDesc: Everyone else is padding. Your star is "efficient output."
entries.quote: 50 burger? Sure. Open the garbage-time filter and let's talk.
entries.longDesc:
One of your deepest joys in life is delivering the verdict: stat-padded. You drop that phrase so often it could be trademarked.

Triple-double? "Padded in garbage time." 40? "Green light all night, you call that efficient?" Won the game? "Teammates set him up." Lost the game? "Empty-calorie scorer, confirmed." You inspect every box score under your padding microscope — except your guy's. Same numbers, different name, suddenly it's "efficient output" and "clutch production." Your double standard runs like a ref's whistle: precise and biased.

Your career moment was Russ's averaged-triple-double season. You finally had the perfect target. You wrote no fewer than five threads on "Westbrook Triple-Double Audit," armed with On/Off splits, teammate rebound rates, garbage-time share — seven angles deep. The analysis was real. The motivation was glaringly visible.

You aren't low-BBIQ. You simply channel all of it into one niche craft: nitpicking other people's résumés. If that were a paying job, you'd already be a principal at a firm.
entries.svgPortrait: A person holding a giant magnifying glass, inspecting a scoreboard packed with player stats. Expression: stern, picky — a quality inspector scanning for defects. Around him float red labels reading "padded," "empty," "fake stats." Behind him two comparison boxes labeled "him" (X'd out) and "my guy" (checkmark), with identical numbers. Palette: audit red, warning yellow, cold gray background.
entries.portraitPrompt: Satirical comic-style stat auditor, giant magnifier over scoreboard, red 'padded' and 'empty' labels floating, two side-by-side comparison boxes with identical stats but opposite verdicts, audit red and warning yellow palette.

entries.name: The 'Called It' Oracle
entries.shortDesc: Your picks hit often enough that it's weird — and you will pull up the chat log to prove it.
entries.quote: I called this a week ago. Scroll up.
entries.longDesc:
You're not just a fan, you're a pre-game spoiler. Final margins, series arcs, who no-shows in the clutch — you land just enough of these that pulling up the receipts feels like submitting evidence.

What drives people nuts is you actually are kind of good. Not every time, but the hits get screenshotted instantly and the misses get deleted even faster. You're amateur probability — showcasing only the positive samples, concealing the negative. Your Instagram story archive is a tightly-curated track record that looks flawless.

Every time you say "told you so," the chat wants to mute you. But at the same time, they're low-key curious: how'd you get this one? You enjoy the weird tension of being annoying and needed — a folk prophet without a temple, just a group chat.

You see yourself as basketball's Paul the Octopus. Your friends see you more as that guy at the casino who wins three hands in a row and starts giving life advice. The truth's probably in between — leaning slightly toward your side.
entries.svgPortrait: A person sitting in front of their phone, which shows a pre-game prediction message validated by the final score, big green check next to it. Mysterious smug half-smile, cheek propped on one hand. Behind them: a crystal-ball-shaped basketball and several bracket grids. Table covered in prediction scribbles. Palette: mysterious purple, oracle gold, phone-screen white.
entries.portraitPrompt: Comic-style oracle fan, seated at phone showing validated prediction, smug mystery smile, crystal-ball basketball and bracket grid backdrop, prediction notes scattered, purple and gold.

entries.name: The Finals-Only Fan
entries.shortDesc: Visible only during the Finals. Silent the other 11 months.
entries.quote: Is #23 LeBron or Jordan?
entries.longDesc:
Your basketball lifecycle: hibernate → tournament → chirp → vanish → repeat. You're the cicada of fandom. Only audible in peak heat.

The Finals start. Your profile picture becomes a superstar. Stories begin featuring "who's winning?" captions over stock arena photos. You start dropping "day-one fan of [franchise]" in the comments. The shield stays up until someone asks the wrong question — like who their starting point guard is — and the entire costume drops.

You aren't malicious. You're just extraordinarily skilled at overconfident play when you should be quiet. Drake and his curse are comedy gold to you, but honestly, your grasp of the rulebook is maybe one "traveling" deep past his.

The night the trophy is lifted, you post "[CHAMPION] on top, been here since day one!" — and then you go silent for eleven months, until June returns. Your basketball engagement graph is a near-flat EKG with one spike in the middle, over and over again.

Real fans grind their teeth. But the league's business model literally depends on you — without Finals-only fans, the ratings bump isn't what it is. In your own weird way, you are a pillar. Just a pillar in the very last row.
entries.svgPortrait: A person in a still-creased brand-new champion tee, fumbling for a phone photo of the TV. Phone search bar visible: "STARTING 5 2026 [TEAM]." Around him, brand new team merch with tags still on. TV shows Finals footage. Expression: trying to fake knowing, eyes betraying confusion. Palette: fresh-out-the-box white, TV blue glow, embarrassed pink flush.
entries.portraitPrompt: Comedic comic-style fake fan, crease-new champion tee photographing TV, search bar open to roster question, tagged merch nearby, TV shows Finals, bluffing expression, fresh white and TV blue with nervous pink.

entries.name: The Vibes Captain
entries.shortDesc: The game is secondary. The post has to hit. The groupchat has to pop.
entries.quote: I'M IN!!! WHO'S UP!!! WE GOTTA GET THIS ONE!!!
entries.longDesc:
Your top metric for a broadcast isn't content, it's vibe. Let the film session happen later — right now, Stories, groupchats, reels, and arena chants all need to be handled in real time. You are the fandom's portable megaphone. Even if you caught only half the action, you will still drag the room to max volume.

Your watching loop: big play → screenshot → caption → post → check engagement → return to game. Loop takes three minutes, which means you've missed three more big plays. Your feed is more dramatic than the actual game. That's fine, that's the brand.

Inside the groupchat you arrive like a can of caffeine cracked open: "HE'S ON THE WING!!! THIS BOUT TO BE NASTY!!! HE DID IT!!!" Your messages are law — three exclamation points minimum, bonus allcaps for weekends. People call it loud. But also — a groupchat without you feels a bit empty.

You aren't oblivious to basketball. You've just decided "knowing the game" and "being lit about the game" are two different jobs, and you applied for the second one. Basketball is, at its root, joy. You are a joy engine. Sometimes also a noise source.
entries.svgPortrait: A person in eye-popping jersey stands in the middle of a crowd, one hand livestreaming on a phone, the other hoisting a handmade sign. Around them: chat text, exclamation points, emojis, cheer effects. Mouth wide open mid-scream. Background is a blurred energetic crowd. Palette: high-saturation rainbow, energy orange, party vibes.
entries.portraitPrompt: Energetic comic-style hype fan, loud jersey in center of crowd, streaming on phone and waving sign, surrounded by chat bubbles exclamation emojis cheer effects, mouth wide open shouting, saturated rainbow plus energy orange.

entries.name: The Zen Spectator
entries.shortDesc: Win? "Mm." Lose? "Mm." Walk-off three? "Oh, it fell." You have transcended.
entries.quote: It'll be what it'll be. If I catch the game, I catch it. If not, no harm done.
entries.longDesc:
You watch like you're on a cushion in a monastery. Win? Don't hoot. Lose? Don't fume. Walk-off three on you? "Oh, it went in." It's not that you don't have feelings — it's that a decade of watching your team has trained those feelings into a very flat sine wave. Others see enlightenment. You know it's scar tissue dressed up nicely.

You've been a fan of this team for more than ten years. In that span: four coaches, three core trades, two "this time it's different" rebuilds, and zero (or one quickly extinguished) playoff runs. Your emotional curve has compressed from a rollercoaster into a straight line. Your heart didn't die. It installed noise-cancellation.

Ask how the game was: "eh, alright." Tell you the buzzer-beater just buried them: "mm." Pitch you a trade rumor with genuine excitement: "yeah, we'll see what the front office does." You respond like a ten-year-old home AC — still on, barely cooling.

But you haven't left. You still watch. You still check scores. You've just stopped tying your mood to the box score. Whether this is wisdom or surrender is a question you've stopped trying to resolve.
entries.svgPortrait: A person cross-legged on the couch, a cup of tea nearby. TV shows a walk-off buzzer-beater — scoreboard flipping, crowd erupting — but his expression is serene, even slightly sleepy. Phone beside him is dark. Moon visible through the window. He wears a soft, very old team tee. Palette: minimal gray-blue, warm tea tones, loud TV moment contrasting quietly.
entries.portraitPrompt: Minimalist comic-style zen fan, cross-legged on couch watching serenely, buzzer-beater on TV, tea beside him, dark phone, old team tee, moonlight outside, gray-blue palette against the loud TV moment.

entries.name: The League Pass ATM
entries.shortDesc: Six-figure fandom. The merch department's favorite kind of devotion.
entries.quote: This isn't spending. It's topping up the faith account.
entries.longDesc:
You express loyalty with your wallet — and you express it loud.

Authentic jerseys? Two per season minimum, home and road, city edition if it goes hard. Sneakers? The AJ shelf is full, you're evaluating a second shelf. Trading cards? You research Panini and Upper Deck grading with the diligence of someone quoting a bond. Courtside? Once a year, because anywhere else is a waste of your time and the resolution.

Your love for basketball is fully measurable — in US dollars. Add up the last five years and you're essentially one mid-SUV into it. Every purchase you explain to yourself as "investing in what I love." Your credit card statement reads more like a luxury-tax worksheet for the league.

Your apartment is a private museum: framed signed jerseys on the wall, sneaker vitrines, display cases of rookie cards, two signed basketballs on the desk (one to show off, one never touched). Your mom/partner periodically asks, "what exactly is the point?" Your stock answer is "you don't get it."

Without people like you, entire merch departments would furlough immediately. You aren't consuming — you're employment security. The league owes you a lifetime achievement award.
entries.svgPortrait: A person standing in front of a wall of framed jerseys, a sneaker display case, and rookie card cabinets — essentially a private museum. Holding a game ticket and a credit card, proud. Several unopened shoe boxes and shipping boxes at his feet. Palette: gold display lighting, dark wood cases, basketball orange highlights.
entries.portraitPrompt: Glam comic-style collector fan, in front of framed jerseys, sneaker cases, rookie card cabinets, holding ticket and credit card, proud, unopened shoe boxes at feet, gold lighting and dark wood.

entries.name: The Pickup Legend (Self-Declared)
entries.shortDesc: "I got that shot." Then: airball.
entries.quote: I could hit that. (0.3 seconds later) Wind. Definitely wind.
entries.longDesc:
Your favorite phrase is "I could hit that." Fate's favorite reply is "airball."

You don't just watch, you hoop. You're a park regular in compression sleeves and an NBA-branded sock tucked up your calf, with your own pump and a microfiber towel. Your gear is several tiers above your game. Your warmup routine is a full production — crossover, behind-the-back, pull-up jumper — and by the time it's done, the other team is respectfully bracing. First possession: airball. Respect: lifted.

You value yourself well above market. Your self-assessment lives somewhere near G-League bubble. Your actual pickup level might not crack the top-three of the court. That doesn't stop you from running live commentary on NBA games: "his crossover's too slow," "I could make that pass," "that help-side gap, I'd drive it." Your partner has heard the monologue enough times they just stare at you like "go on, do it."

The lovable-and-punchable thing is that even when you got posterized in last week's run, this week, watching the league, you still say "I could hit that." There's something Mamba about that self-belief — except Mamba hit his. You didn't.

Still, you show up every week. You still believe you're improving. Maybe you are. The improvement is real. Just about ten times slower than your commentary implies.
entries.svgPortrait: A person on a blacktop park mid-follow-through of an exaggerated step-back fadeaway, off one foot, radiating confidence. The ball's arc is obviously off, heading the wrong way, miles from the rim. A friend behind them is cracking up with their head in their hands. Passersby film it. He's wearing full compression sleeves, knee braces, branded socks — gear stacked. Palette: concrete gray, sky blue, comedic warm yellow.
entries.portraitPrompt: Exaggerated comic-style pickup-legend, exaggerated step-back fade missing badly, friend laughing behind, passersby filming, overgeared in sleeves and braces, park court, concrete gray and sky blue, comedic warm yellow light.

entries.name: The Team Mom
entries.shortDesc: You don't follow basketball — you worry about a roster of kids you never raised.
entries.quote: He's twenty-two. Don't push so hard. No title deadline this year.
entries.longDesc:
You don't follow basketball, you parent. A player's minutes climb, you worry he's tired. Small tweak? You hurt more than he does. The words "playing through injury" make you quietly freak out. You treat NBA players exactly like you'd treat your own kid at a track meet — results are a bonus, health is the priority, and "don't push him" is the underlying worldview.

Under every injury tweet: "Take your time, rest up, seasons come back." Under every young-player slump post: "He's [age]. Give him time." Under every retirement announcement: "Thank you for the years."

You're the thermostat of the fan base. When everyone's flaming a missed shot, a coach's brain, the GM's soul, you're the one asking, "Is he okay? Has he been sleeping? He's been playing a lot of minutes lately."

Real hoops Twitter finds you soft. You counter that the game is, in the end, played by people, and people come first. You also quietly understand that voicing this in an NBA Reddit megathread gets you roasted as "his mother." The roast doesn't land — you just reply, "even if he isn't my kid, he's somebody's."

You're not built for hot-take sparring. You're built for the league's player-mental-health initiative.
entries.svgPortrait: A person wrapped in a team scarf, holding up a team sign in one hand, the other hand gently pressed to their chest. Expression: soft worry — not for the standings, for his knees. TV shows a player hitting the floor in slow motion. A mug of hot tea and a symbolic bandaid on the coffee table. Palette: warm beige, soft anxious blue, team color accents.
entries.portraitPrompt: Tender comic-style worried fan, wrapped in team scarf hand on chest watching slow-mo player fall on TV, hot tea and symbolic bandaid on table, parental concern expression, warm beige and soft blue.

entries.name: Basketball Switzerland
entries.shortDesc: "I'll watch whatever" / "Good with anyone" — perpetual neutral state.
entries.quote: Pick whatever. I'll watch.
entries.longDesc:
Your defining feature in basketball is that you have no defining features. Which team? Whatever. Which game? Whatever. Who do you like? "They all seem fine." Who do you hate? "No strong feelings." You are basketball's Switzerland — perpetually neutral, perpetually calm, perpetually un-opinionated.

Living this way is, to be fair, peaceful. No gut-punch losses, no elimination meltdowns, no buzzer-beater heartbreak. But also: no title ecstasy, no overtime scream, no superstar-cracking-out-of-his-shell moment of pure interior fireworks. You've converted basketball into a polite companion activity — like a fitness app you installed and never opened. It exists. It doesn't move you.

Friends invite you to a game. You go. They ask who you're pulling for. "Either." They ask who wins. "Hard to call." You produce "both sides have a case" analysis, mostly because you didn't do any analysis. You hide "no opinion" behind "I'm neutral," and "I don't care" behind "whatever."

You're the guy who went undrafted — not a physical fail, just nobody going "yes, him." Your basketball stance is the same: decent, but no reason to commit.

Your presence evokes mild grief among true fans. You have the time and means to follow — and you're spending it on "whatever." It's like being handed a Finals ticket and falling asleep in your seat.
entries.svgPortrait: A person in a plain jacket with no team logo sitting in the exact middle of a couch. To each side, scarves and merch from two different teams, neither picked up. Expression calm and untroubled, remote in hand, ready to change the channel at any moment. TV plays a game, but he could just as easily be watching anything else. Palette: neutral gray, scattered team colors that never dominate, soft natural light.
entries.portraitPrompt: Neutral comic-style laid-back fan, plain no-logo jacket seated center couch, both team scarves flanking but untouched, remote in hand calm expression, neutral gray palette with scattered team colors that don't dominate.
